front: Concept: a three-word slogan meaning humans have no inherent value but must create it, coined in a 1946 lecture declaring existentialism to be 'a Humanism'
Work: uses the example of a paper-knife, whose 'formulae and qualities' make 'definition possible,' to contrast with human existence
Work: uses a student who must choose between going to war and caring for his bereaved mother to illustrate 'abandonment'
Work: begins by responding to Communists' accusations of quietism, then rehabilitates the ideas of 'abandonment' and 'anguish'
Work: originally delivered as a lecture at the Club Maintenant
Work: claims the morality of Maggie Tulliver in The Mill on the Floss has the same origin as the desire of La Sanseverina in The Charterhouse of Parma
Work: names Karl Jaspers and Gabriel Marcel as the Christian proponents of the philosophy it defends
Work: Heidegger responded to this 1946 lecture in a famous letter to his student Jean Beaufret, claiming the author had misunderstood his work
Work: modifies 'existence' to 'ek-sistenz' (second letter K, hyphen), referring to man's 'standing out' into the truth of 'being' and 'dwelling' in language
Work: opens with 'We are far from understanding...the essence of action'
Philosopher: contrasted the 'spiritual' anxiety of emptiness in the modern era with 'ontic' anxiety of death in late antiquity and 'moral' anxiety of damnation in the late Middle Ages
Philosopher: this Christian existentialist described himself as a 'philosopher of existence,' claimed 'we live in a broken world and require transcendence,' and wrote The Mystery of Being
Concept: in Being and Nothingness, a cafe waiter whose movements are 'too precise' illustrates this two-word concept
Philosopher: Lewis Gordon, a scholar in this thinker's tradition, wrote a book pairing bad faith with Antiblack Racism
Work: Walter Kaufmann's history of existentialism, subtitled 'from Dostoevsky to Sartre,' examines the core question 'What is Transcendence?'
Work: William Barrett's study of existentialism concludes with an analysis of 'the place of the furies'
Philosopher: derived his concept of ontological security from existentialist readings; wrote The Divided Self
Philosopher: drew on existentialism to criticize artificial intelligence in What Computers Can't Do
